cards.prompt: Our core banking system has been rock-solid for 30 years. Honestly, we're a bit afraid to touch it.
cards.rootCause: Undocumented COBOL; the original authors have retired or left.
cards.consequence: Blocks every modern initiative; acute key-person risk if the few who understand it leave.
cards.diagnostic: If the two people who understand it best left tomorrow, what happens to your ability to change it?
cards.angle: Agents read and document what humans no longer can — incrementally, under human review, no risky big-bang rewrite.
cards.objection: You can't seriously let an AI touch our core ledger.
cards.reframe: Agreed — and we don't. It reads and documents to make the system legible first; any change goes through your existing review gates. We start with understanding, not surgery.

personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of maintaining an opaque system
personaShift.VPE: reduces risk on every change to the core

cards.soWhat: Fear guards every legacy core. Start read-only: understanding before surgery is what earns the first yes.

cards.prompt: We'd love to move off the mainframe but the last team that scoped it quoted five years and eight figures.
cards.rootCause: Manual translation of millions of lines is prohibitively slow and expensive.
cards.consequence: Permanent deferral; compounding maintenance cost and rising risk.
cards.diagnostic: What's your annual run-cost just to keep it alive — and how much of that is people versus licensing?
cards.angle: Agents compress the translation timeline and de-risk it by working incrementally against a verifiable target.
cards.objection: We've heard 'faster migration' promises before and got burned.
cards.reframe: Fair. That's why we scope one bounded slice first, agree a success metric, and prove it before anyone commits to the program. You buy the outcome, not the promise.

personaShift.CFO: turns an eight-figure program into an incremental, measurable spend
personaShift.CTO: makes the impossible roadmap item tractable
personaShift.VPE: reduces risk on every change to the core

cards.soWhat: When a rewrite feels impossibly huge, shrink the ask — one bounded, measurable slice beats an eight-figure leap of faith.

cards.prompt: Everything critical runs through a green-screen system nobody under 50 knows how to operate.
cards.rootCause: Decades-old transaction system with a shrinking pool of skilled operators.
cards.consequence: Operational fragility; institutional knowledge walking out the door.
cards.diagnostic: What's the oldest system you're most afraid to lose the last expert on?
cards.angle: Agents capture the logic and generate documentation before that knowledge is gone.
cards.objection: That knowledge is too specialized for a tool to capture.
cards.reframe: It won't replace your expert's judgment — it captures the mechanical logic they carry so the next person isn't starting from zero.

personaShift.VPE: shortens onboarding from months to weeks
personaShift.CFO: cuts the run-cost of an opaque legacy system

cards.soWhat: Key-person risk is a countdown, not a maybe. Sell the urgency of capturing logic before the last expert walks out.

cards.prompt: Our regulator would never accept AI-generated changes to a core system.
cards.rootCause: Assumption that agentic work bypasses human control.
cards.consequence: Modernization stalls under perceived compliance risk.
cards.diagnostic: What would an AI-assisted change need to satisfy to pass your existing control review?
cards.angle: The agent is a constrained identity — every change is a reviewable PR through the same controls any contributor uses.
cards.objection: Our regulator would never accept AI-generated changes.
cards.reframe: They don't have to accept anything new. The change is proposed by the agent but approved by your engineer and passes your existing gates — the control model is unchanged. The agent is just a faster contributor inside it.

personaShift.CRO: operates within existing CPS 234/230 control environment
personaShift.CTO: no new governance surface to build
personaShift.VPE: reduces risk on every change to the core
personaShift.CFO: cuts the run-cost of an opaque legacy system

cards.soWhat: Don't pitch new governance. Show the agent riding existing controls: reviewable PRs, same gates, nothing new to trust.

cards.prompt: We've wrapped our mainframe in so many workarounds that the workarounds are now the problem.
cards.rootCause: Layers of middleware and patches built around an untouchable core.
cards.consequence: Fragile integration web; every change risks cascading failure.
cards.diagnostic: How many systems would you have to test if you changed one thing in the core today?
cards.angle: Agents map the integration web and document dependencies so change becomes safe and scoped.
cards.objection: The integrations are too tangled for anything to safely map.
cards.reframe: Tangled is exactly what agents are good at tracing — they follow the dependencies methodically and surface the map no single person holds anymore.

personaShift.VPE: reduces risk on every change to the core
personaShift.CFO: cuts the run-cost of an opaque legacy system

cards.soWhat: When the workarounds become the problem, sell the map first — you can't safely change what nobody can see.

cards.prompt: Our mainframe team says an AI can't possibly understand COBOL business logic.
cards.rootCause: Domain experts doubt the agent can grasp decades of embedded logic.
cards.consequence: Internal resistance blocks adoption even where value is clear.
cards.diagnostic: What would it take for your mainframe team to believe it — a small proof on a module they know cold?
cards.angle: Prove it on a module the experts know intimately, so they judge the output against their own understanding.
cards.objection: An AI can't possibly understand our COBOL business logic.
cards.reframe: Let's not argue it — let's test it. Point it at a module your team knows cold and judge the documentation it produces against your own understanding. The experts become the verifiers, not the skeptics.

personaShift.CFO: cuts the run-cost of an opaque legacy system

cards.soWhat: Never argue capability. Let skeptics test it on a module they know cold and judge the output themselves.

cards.prompt: Our insurance policy admin still runs on a 1980s mainframe and every quote goes through it.
cards.pattern: Core policy system on legacy iron
cards.rootCause: Business-critical COBOL policy engine nobody wants to touch.
cards.consequence: New products take months because the core can't flex.
cards.diagnostic: How long does it take you to launch a new policy type through that system today?
cards.angle: Agents document the policy rules so you can extend the core safely instead of freezing it.
cards.objection: That engine is too central to risk experimenting on.
cards.reframe: That's why we start read-only — documenting the rules, changing nothing — so you gain understanding before any edit.

personaShift.CTO: unblocks product velocity on the core
personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of a rigid policy engine
personaShift.CRO: reduces key-person risk on a critical system

cards.soWhat: 'Too central to touch' is the opening, not the objection — read-only documentation changes nothing and earns entry.

cards.prompt: The whole billing run is a nightly batch of JCL jobs held together by scripts from the 90s.
cards.rootCause: Undocumented job chains that only a couple of operators understand.
cards.consequence: A single failed job can delay billing for the whole business.
cards.diagnostic: When a batch job fails at 2am, who's the one person you call?
cards.angle: Agents map and document the job dependencies so the pipeline stops being a black box.
cards.objection: Those job chains are too fragile to let anything analyze them.
cards.reframe: Analysis is read-only — the agent traces the dependencies without running or changing anything, giving you the map you're missing.

personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of operational fragility

cards.soWhat: A black box everyone fears is a documentation sale. Trace the job chains before the 2am call finds no one.

cards.prompt: Our airline's crew-scheduling system is Assembler that predates most of the team.
cards.rootCause: Low-level legacy code with almost no living expertise.
cards.consequence: Operational scheduling is a fragile black box.
cards.diagnostic: If that system went down for a day, what's the operational cost to the airline?
cards.angle: Agents reconstruct and document the logic from the code itself, even in Assembler.
cards.objection: Assembler is too low-level for any tool to make sense of.
cards.reframe: Low-level is tedious for humans but tractable for an agent that reads it methodically and documents the intent behind it.

personaShift.CTO: recovers understanding of a critical system
personaShift.CFO: reduces the cost of a fragile core

cards.soWhat: What's tedious for humans is tractable for agents. Reframe 'too low-level' as exactly why they need the help.

cards.prompt: State tax processing runs on PL/I that's been patched for forty years.
cards.rootCause: Decades of accreted PL/I changes with no coherent documentation.
cards.consequence: Every legislative change is a high-risk scramble.
cards.diagnostic: How confident are you that you know everything that code does today?
cards.angle: Agents produce the documentation that makes annual legislative changes safe and fast.
cards.objection: Public-sector systems can't take that kind of risk.
cards.reframe: Understanding first reduces risk — the agent documents before anything changes, so your legislative updates get safer, not riskier.

personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of high-risk manual change

cards.soWhat: In risk-averse shops, safety is the yes you're selling — lead with 'understand before you change,' not speed.

cards.prompt: Our warehouse still runs on an AS/400 with RPG programs from the original build.
cards.rootCause: Green-screen RPG core with a retiring operator base.
cards.consequence: Warehouse operations depend on a shrinking pool of skills.
cards.diagnostic: What's the average age of the people who can still maintain that RPG code?
cards.angle: Agents document the RPG logic and open a path off the green screen.
cards.objection: IBM i is a niche stack nothing supports.
cards.reframe: The agent reads the RPG and business logic regardless of niche — what matters is capturing the logic before the last operator retires.

personaShift.CTO: opens a modernization path for IBM i
personaShift.VPE: cuts onboarding time on a niche stack
personaShift.CFO: reduces reliance on scarce RPG skills

cards.soWhat: Niche stacks aren't the blocker. The sale is capturing logic before a retiring operator base takes it with them.

cards.prompt: We can't change the mainframe because we genuinely don't know what depends on it downstream.
cards.rootCause: No dependency map; fear of breaking unknown consumers freezes the core.
cards.consequence: The core is effectively frozen — no change is considered safe.
cards.diagnostic: If you changed one field in the core record today, could you list everything that would break?
cards.angle: Agents trace the downstream dependencies and produce the impact map that unlocks safe change.
cards.objection: The dependencies are too spread out to ever fully map.
cards.reframe: Spread out is exactly what an agent handles — it follows the data across systems methodically where no single person can hold it all.

personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of a frozen core

cards.soWhat: Fear of the unknown freezes the core. Sell the impact map that finally answers 'what breaks if I change this?'

cards.prompt: Our COBOL is fine — the real problem is the copybooks and data definitions nobody fully understands.
cards.rootCause: Shared copybooks and record layouts drifted beyond anyone's full grasp.
cards.consequence: Data changes ripple unpredictably across programs.
cards.diagnostic: How many programs share your most-used copybook, and who tracks that?
cards.angle: Agents map copybook usage and data lineage across the estate.
cards.objection: The copybook sprawl is decades deep.
cards.reframe: Depth is why humans gave up on it — an agent traces every reference systematically and gives you the lineage back.

personaShift.CTO: restores control over shared data definitions
personaShift.CFO: reduces the cost of unpredictable ripple effects

cards.soWhat: The problem often isn't the COBOL — it's the data nobody tracks. Sell lineage where humans already gave up.

cards.prompt: Every attempt to move off the mainframe stalls because we can't freeze requirements long enough.
cards.rootCause: The legacy system keeps changing, so the migration target never stabilizes.
cards.consequence: Migrations chase a moving system and never converge.
cards.diagnostic: How much does the mainframe change in a typical quarter while you're trying to migrate off it?
cards.angle: Agents keep the migrated target in sync as the source evolves, closing the gap continuously.
cards.objection: By the time we migrate a module, it's already changed.
cards.reframe: That's the case for continuous agent-driven migration — it re-syncs against the current source rather than a frozen snapshot.
cards.soWhat: If the target keeps moving, sell continuous re-sync, not a frozen snapshot — that's why past migrations never converged.

cards.prompt: The mainframe MIPS bill is our biggest infrastructure line and it keeps growing.
cards.rootCause: Inefficient legacy code and growing volume drive rising compute charges.
cards.consequence: A large, escalating cost with no clear lever to reduce it.
cards.diagnostic: What's driving the MIPS growth — volume, or inefficient code paths you could optimize?
cards.angle: Agents identify and optimize the hot code paths, and enable offload of workloads to cheaper platforms.
cards.objection: MIPS cost is just the price of running a mainframe.
cards.reframe: Some of it is — but a lot is inefficient code and workloads that don't need to be there. Agents can find and address both.

personaShift.CFO: attacks the largest infra line item
personaShift.CRO: reduces concentration on a single costly platform

cards.soWhat: Separate the cost of doing business from waste. Some compute growth is inefficiency an agent can find and cut.

cards.prompt: We modernized the front end but the mainframe core underneath is unchanged and still limits us.
cards.rootCause: UI was refreshed while the constraining core logic stayed put.
cards.consequence: New interfaces still inherit the core's limitations.
cards.diagnostic: Which of your current product constraints actually trace back to the core, not the UI?
cards.angle: Agents tackle the core logic so modernization goes deeper than the surface.
cards.objection: We already modernized — the new UI proves it.
cards.reframe: The UI is real progress, but if products still stall on the core's rules, that's where the next modernization has to reach.

personaShift.CTO: extends modernization to the real constraint
personaShift.CFO: protects the UI investment by fixing the root
personaShift.CRO: reduces risk concentrated in the core

cards.soWhat: A new UI over an old core still inherits its limits. Sell modernization that reaches the real constraint.

cards.prompt: Our brokerage settlement runs on the mainframe and the logic is spread across hundreds of programs.
cards.rootCause: Settlement rules scattered across many programs with no single owner.
cards.consequence: Nobody can confidently reason about the end-to-end flow.
cards.diagnostic: Could anyone on your team draw the full settlement flow end to end today?
cards.angle: Agents trace the flow across all the programs and assemble a coherent end-to-end view.
cards.objection: The logic is too fragmented to reconstruct.
cards.reframe: Fragmentation is the exact problem agents solve — they stitch the scattered pieces into the coherent picture no person holds.

personaShift.CTO: recovers an end-to-end view of settlement

cards.soWhat: When no one can draw the flow end to end, that's the sale — agents stitch scattered logic into one picture.

cards.prompt: We know the mainframe is a risk but leadership won't fund modernization without a hard business case.
cards.rootCause: Risk is real but hasn't been translated into numbers leadership will fund.
cards.consequence: The initiative stalls in every budget cycle.
cards.diagnostic: What would it take, in numbers, for your board to treat this as urgent rather than someday?
cards.angle: Agents make the first bounded slice cheap enough to prove ROI and build the business case with real data.
cards.objection: We can't justify the spend without proof it works.
cards.reframe: Then don't justify the whole program — fund one bounded slice, measure it, and let the result build the business case for the rest.

personaShift.CFO: turns a vague risk into a measured ROI case
personaShift.CRO: converts latent risk into a fundable plan

cards.soWhat: Don't sell the program, sell the proof. One cheap slice with real numbers builds the case leadership will fund.

cards.prompt: Our COBOL developers are retiring and we're paying contractors triple to keep the lights on.
cards.rootCause: Scarce COBOL skills command premium rates as the talent pool shrinks.
cards.consequence: Run-cost rises every year with no end in sight.
cards.diagnostic: What are you paying per contractor-day now versus five years ago — and where does that curve go?
cards.angle: Agents carry the maintenance load, cutting dependence on scarce, expensive COBOL contractors.
cards.objection: Even agents need someone who understands COBOL to supervise.
cards.reframe: Yes — one supervisor reviewing agent output, not a team of premium contractors doing the grind. That's the cost shift.

personaShift.CTO: reduces dependence on a shrinking talent pool

cards.soWhat: Shift the cost story: one reviewer over agent output, not a team of premium contractors doing the grind.

cards.prompt: We want to expose mainframe data to modern apps but every integration is a custom project.
cards.rootCause: No clean interface; each new consumer requires bespoke plumbing.
cards.consequence: Every modern initiative pays a mainframe-integration tax.
cards.diagnostic: How long does it take to stand up a new integration to the core today?
cards.angle: Agents document the interfaces and help build reusable access layers so integration stops being bespoke.
cards.objection: The core wasn't built to be integrated with cleanly.
cards.reframe: It wasn't — which is why documenting what's there and building a reusable layer, rather than another one-off, is the leverage point.

personaShift.CTO: removes the integration tax on new work
personaShift.CRO: reduces risk from ad-hoc access paths

cards.soWhat: Every bespoke integration is a recurring tax. Sell the reusable layer, not another one-off you'll pay for again.

cards.prompt: Auditors keep asking us to prove what our mainframe programs actually do and we can't.
cards.rootCause: No current documentation of what the programs do or why.
cards.consequence: Audit findings recur; evidence is assembled manually each time.
cards.diagnostic: How much effort goes into re-explaining these programs to auditors every cycle?
cards.angle: Agents generate accurate, current documentation that serves as durable audit evidence.
cards.objection: Auditors want authoritative documentation, not machine-generated notes.
cards.reframe: The agent's output is reviewed and signed off by your engineers before it's authoritative — it removes the manual drafting, not the human authority.

personaShift.CTO: makes the core auditable on demand

cards.soWhat: Recurring audit pain is durable value — agent-drafted, human-signed docs turn re-explaining into standing evidence.

cards.prompt: We're merging with another bank and both cores are on different mainframe stacks.
cards.rootCause: Two undocumented cores must be understood before they can be consolidated.
cards.consequence: Integration risk is enormous and the timeline is unforgiving.
cards.diagnostic: Which of the two cores does anyone actually understand well enough to consolidate onto?
cards.angle: Agents document both cores fast so the consolidation decision rests on real understanding.
cards.objection: We don't have time to fully understand both before we have to act.
cards.reframe: That's why speed of understanding matters — agents document both in parallel, far faster than a manual discovery, so the decision isn't a guess.
cards.soWhat: Under a merger clock, speed of understanding is the sale — document both cores in parallel so the call isn't a guess.

cards.prompt: Our head of ops says any mainframe project always overruns, so why would this be different.
cards.rootCause: History of large mainframe programs blowing budget and timeline.
cards.consequence: Deep skepticism toward any new modernization effort.
cards.diagnostic: What did those past overruns have in common — scope, or the big-bang approach?
cards.angle: Bounded, incremental slices with a proven metric replace the big-bang pattern that caused the overruns.
cards.objection: Mainframe projects always overrun.
cards.reframe: They overrun when they're big-bang. We invert that — one small slice, measured, proven, before any commitment. You never fund the overrun-shaped version.

personaShift.CFO: caps downside to one bounded slice
personaShift.VPE: delivers value before overrun risk builds

cards.soWhat: 'Always overruns' really means 'always big-bang.' Sell the inverted shape: one measured slice before any commitment.

cards.prompt: Security says giving any automated identity access to the mainframe is a non-starter.
cards.rootCause: Assumption that an agent needs broad, unmanaged access.
cards.consequence: Security posture blocks adoption on the highest-value system.
cards.diagnostic: What access boundary would satisfy security for any new contributor to the core?
cards.angle: The agent operates as a constrained, scoped identity inside your existing access controls — nothing new to trust.
cards.objection: We won't grant automated access to the mainframe.
cards.reframe: It gets the same scoped, audited access any contributor gets — no more. The control model doesn't change; a constrained identity operates inside it.

personaShift.CFO: avoids the cost of a bespoke control regime

cards.soWhat: Don't ask for new access. Show the agent as a scoped, audited identity inside controls that already exist.

cards.prompt: Our COBOL veterans feel this is a threat to the expertise they've built over decades.
cards.rootCause: Veterans see the agent as devaluing hard-won expertise.
cards.consequence: Passive resistance from the people whose buy-in you most need.
cards.diagnostic: What work would your veterans rather be doing than the maintenance grind they're stuck in?
cards.angle: Veterans become the reviewers and architects; the agent takes the grind, elevating their role rather than replacing it.
cards.objection: This will make our COBOL experts feel obsolete.
cards.reframe: It makes them the authority — they review and direct the agent's work. Their judgment becomes the leverage point, not the bottleneck.

personaShift.VPE: elevates veterans to reviewers and mentors
personaShift.CFO: gets more value from expensive experts
personaShift.CRO: reduces flight risk of key people

cards.soWhat: Threatened experts sink deals quietly. Sell elevation — they direct and review while the agent takes the grind.

cards.prompt: We tried a mainframe code-analysis tool years ago and the output was useless.
cards.rootCause: Earlier static tools produced shallow output nobody could act on.
cards.consequence: Skepticism that any tool can meaningfully understand the core.
cards.diagnostic: What made that earlier output useless — was it wrong, or just not actionable?
cards.angle: Agents produce reviewable, actionable understanding — and you judge it on a module your team knows before trusting it further.
cards.objection: We tried a code-analysis tool and it was useless.
cards.reframe: Static analysis describes syntax; an agent reasons about intent and produces something your engineers can actually act on. Test it on a known module and judge for yourself.

personaShift.VPE: output the team can actually use
personaShift.CFO: avoids repeating a wasted tool spend

cards.soWhat: Past burn is skepticism, not a no. Distinguish reasoning from static analysis, then let them test it on known code.

cards.prompt: Our pension calculations run on a mainframe system last meaningfully updated in 1998.
cards.rootCause: Critical calculations locked in code no one dares modify.
cards.consequence: Rule changes are deferred or bolted on unsafely outside the core.
cards.diagnostic: Where do new pension rules actually get implemented if not in the core engine?
cards.angle: Agents document the calculation rules so the engine can be safely extended again.
cards.objection: Those calculations are too sensitive to risk any change.
cards.reframe: Sensitivity is the reason to understand them first — the agent documents the rules read-only before any change is contemplated.
cards.soWhat: 'Too sensitive to change' means sell understanding first — document the rules read-only before anyone contemplates an edit.

cards.prompt: Our utility's meter-to-cash system is IMS and DB2 that predates the current org chart.
cards.rootCause: IMS/DB2 core with logic and structure few still understand.
cards.consequence: Billing changes are slow, risky, and expert-dependent.
cards.diagnostic: Who's the last person who genuinely understands your IMS database structure?
cards.angle: Agents document the IMS structures and access logic before that knowledge is lost.
cards.objection: IMS is an old hierarchical model nobody learns anymore.
cards.reframe: Exactly why capturing it now matters — the agent reads the structure and logic regardless of how unfashionable the model is.

personaShift.CTO: preserves understanding of a critical DB
personaShift.CFO: reduces run-cost of an opaque system

cards.soWhat: Unfashionable models still run the business. Sell capturing the logic now, before the last person who gets it leaves.

cards.prompt: Our mainframe change process is so heavy that even tiny fixes take a full quarter.
cards.rootCause: Fear-driven process wraps every change in heavy manual ceremony.
cards.consequence: Delivery on the core grinds to a crawl.
cards.diagnostic: How much of that quarter is actual work versus process built to compensate for not understanding the code?
cards.angle: Better understanding and automated verification let you safely lighten the ceremony.
cards.objection: The heavy process is what keeps the core safe.
cards.reframe: Much of that process exists because no one trusts their understanding of the code. Restore the understanding and automated tests, and the ceremony can shrink without losing safety.

personaShift.VPE: restores delivery speed on the core
personaShift.CFO: cuts the cost of glacial change

cards.soWhat: Heavy ceremony is distrust of the code in disguise. Restore understanding and tests, and the process can safely shrink.

cards.prompt: We have three generations of code on the mainframe — COBOL, PL/I, and Assembler — all interdependent.
cards.rootCause: Layers written in different eras and languages, tightly coupled.
cards.consequence: No one understands the cross-language interactions.
cards.diagnostic: When something breaks across the COBOL/Assembler boundary, how do you even diagnose it?
cards.angle: Agents trace logic across language boundaries and document the interactions.
cards.objection: The cross-language calls are a black box even to us.
cards.reframe: Agents read all three languages and follow the calls across them — the cross-language tracing humans struggle with is exactly their strength.

personaShift.CRO: reduces risk in a tangled core

cards.soWhat: Cross-language black boxes are an agent's strength, not its limit — sell tracing the boundaries humans can't follow.

cards.prompt: We want to retire the mainframe in five years but have no credible roadmap to get there.
cards.rootCause: A retirement goal exists but no understanding-based plan underpins it.
cards.consequence: The target date is aspirational, not grounded.
cards.diagnostic: What has to be true in year one for the five-year retirement to be real?
cards.angle: Agents build the understanding and incremental migration plan that turns the target into a credible roadmap.
cards.objection: We've set mainframe-retirement dates before and missed them all.
cards.reframe: Missed because they lacked a grounded plan. Start with agent-driven understanding and an incremental first slice, and the date rests on evidence, not hope.

personaShift.CTO: converts a target into a credible roadmap
personaShift.VPE: sequences the work into deliverable slices

cards.soWhat: A date without a plan is hope. Sell the year-one first slice that makes the five-year target actually credible.

cards.prompt: Our mainframe holds decades of business rules that live nowhere else — not even in the specs.
cards.pattern: Code as sole source of truth
cards.rootCause: The only accurate statement of the business rules is the code itself.
cards.consequence: Losing the code understanding means losing the business rules.
cards.diagnostic: If your written specs disagree with the code, which one is actually correct in practice?
cards.angle: Agents extract the real, current business rules from the code into documentation the business can read.
cards.objection: The business rules are too intricate to extract accurately.
cards.reframe: Intricacy is why manual extraction failed — an agent works through every branch systematically and surfaces rules for your experts to confirm.

personaShift.CTO: recovers business rules as durable assets
personaShift.VPE: gives the team a real spec to work from
personaShift.CRO: reduces risk of losing critical rules

cards.soWhat: When specs and code disagree, the code wins. Sell extracting the real rules before that understanding is lost.

cards.prompt: Leadership says if the mainframe still works, spending on it is wasted money.
cards.rootCause: Working today masks the accumulating risk and opportunity cost.
cards.consequence: Investment is withheld until a crisis forces it, at higher cost.
cards.diagnostic: What happens to that stance the day the last person who understands it retires?
cards.angle: Acting while it still works is cheaper and safer than acting in a crisis — the agent makes proactive work affordable.
cards.objection: It works fine, so why spend anything on it?
cards.reframe: It works until the last expert leaves or a rule must change fast — then it's a crisis at a premium. Acting now, cheaply, is the lower-cost path, not the wasteful one.

personaShift.CFO: reframes proactive spend as risk avoidance
personaShift.CRO: closes the window before it becomes a crisis

cards.soWhat: 'It works' masks a countdown. Sell acting now, cheaply, versus acting later in a crisis at a premium.

cards.prompt: Our healthcare claims mainframe was customized so heavily the vendor no longer supports it.
cards.rootCause: Deep customization voided vendor support, leaving you alone with it.
cards.consequence: No vendor safety net; all risk sits with your team.
cards.diagnostic: If a critical bug surfaced tomorrow, who fixes it now that the vendor won't?
cards.angle: Agents document the customizations so your team can own and safely maintain the system.
cards.objection: The customizations are undocumented even by the people who made them.
cards.reframe: The agent reconstructs them from the code — which is now the only record — and gives you back a maintainable, documented system.

personaShift.CTO: restores ownership of an unsupported system
personaShift.CFO: reduces risk of an unsupported core
personaShift.CRO: reduces continuity risk without a vendor

cards.soWhat: No vendor safety net means all the risk is theirs. Sell reconstructing the customizations so they can own it.

cards.prompt: Every year our mainframe modernization gets proposed and every year it loses to revenue projects.
cards.rootCause: Modernization can't compete with revenue work for scarce capacity.
cards.consequence: The risk compounds while the work never starts.
cards.diagnostic: How many years has this lost the priority fight — and what has the risk done in that time?
cards.angle: Agents add capacity so modernization no longer has to win the fight against revenue work.
cards.objection: We simply can't take engineers off revenue for this.
cards.reframe: That's why it never happens. Agents supply the capacity, so modernization proceeds without stealing from revenue projects.
cards.soWhat: If it always loses to revenue work, remove the trade-off — agents add capacity so modernization stops competing.

cards.prompt: Our card-processing switch is decades-old code and it handles every transaction we make.
cards.rootCause: The highest-volume system runs on the least-understood code.
cards.consequence: Maximum business impact concentrated in a fragile core.
cards.diagnostic: What's the blast radius if that switch has an unhandled failure?
cards.angle: Agents document the switch logic to reduce the fragility of your most critical system.
cards.objection: That system is too critical to let anything near it.
cards.reframe: Critical is the reason to understand it, not avoid it — documentation is read-only and reduces exactly the fragility that scares you.

personaShift.CTO: reduces fragility of the top system

cards.soWhat: The most critical system is often the most fragile. Sell documenting the switch logic before a failure forces it.

cards.prompt: We keep hiring mainframe contractors but each one only understands the piece they built.
cards.rootCause: Knowledge fragments across contractors with no shared documentation.
cards.consequence: No one holds the whole picture; every gap is another contract.
cards.diagnostic: Is there a single document that ties together what all these contractors built?
cards.angle: Agents consolidate the fragmented knowledge into one coherent, shared documentation base.
cards.objection: Each contractor's piece is too specialized to unify.
cards.reframe: The agent reads across all the pieces regardless of who built them and produces the unified picture no single contractor has.
cards.soWhat: Fragmented contractor knowledge is a liability you rent. Sell consolidating it into one shared base you own.

cards.prompt: We want to move reporting off the mainframe but the data structures are undocumented.
cards.rootCause: Reporting can't move because the underlying data layout is a mystery.
cards.consequence: Analytics stays trapped on expensive mainframe compute.
cards.diagnostic: What's it costing you to run reporting on mainframe MIPS instead of cheaper compute?
cards.angle: Agents document the data structures so reporting can be safely offloaded to cheaper platforms.
cards.objection: The data layouts are too intricate to safely replicate elsewhere.
cards.reframe: The agent maps the layouts precisely from the code, giving you the documented structure needed to offload with confidence.

personaShift.CRO: reduces reliance on a single platform

cards.soWhat: You can't offload what you can't see. Sell documenting the data so reporting moves to cheaper platforms safely.

cards.prompt: Our board thinks 'AI on the mainframe' sounds reckless and won't approve it.
cards.rootCause: The 'AI touching the core' framing sounds reckless without context.
cards.consequence: Approval stalls on perception, not substance.
cards.diagnostic: Would the board object to a faster contributor whose every change is human-approved and audited?
cards.angle: Reframe from 'AI touches the core' to 'a scoped contributor whose work passes every existing gate.'
cards.objection: The board won't approve AI on our core.
cards.reframe: What we're proposing isn't AI making autonomous changes — it's a scoped assistant proposing changes your engineers approve through existing controls. Framed accurately, it's less risky than the status quo of a shrinking expert pool.
cards.soWhat: Win the board by changing the words: not 'AI touches the core' but 'a scoped contributor passing every gate.'

cards.prompt: The mainframe and our modern systems disagree on data and reconciling them is a full-time job.
cards.rootCause: Undocumented core logic produces data that modern systems can't cleanly reconcile.
cards.consequence: Ongoing manual reconciliation cost and data-trust problems.
cards.diagnostic: How many people spend how much time reconciling core data against your modern systems?
cards.angle: Agents document the core's data logic so reconciliation can be automated or eliminated at the source.
cards.objection: Reconciliation is just unavoidable with two systems.
cards.reframe: It's unavoidable while the core's logic is opaque. Understand it, and the mismatches can be fixed at the source rather than reconciled forever.

personaShift.CTO: fixes data mismatches at the root

cards.soWhat: Reconciliation burden is a symptom of opaque data. Sell documenting the source so the burden shrinks or vanishes.

cards.prompt: Our mainframe modernization needs to show wins fast or it'll lose executive support.
cards.rootCause: Executive patience is short; slow starts kill funding.
cards.consequence: Big-bang plans starve before they deliver anything visible.
cards.diagnostic: What's a win you could put in front of executives within the first month?
cards.angle: Agents deliver a documented, working first slice quickly to build the momentum that sustains funding.
cards.objection: Real mainframe work can't show anything meaningful in a month.
cards.reframe: A bounded slice — a documented module or a small migrated function with passing tests — is real and fast. Early wins are exactly what agent-driven, incremental work produces.

personaShift.CRO: reduces risk of a stalled program

cards.soWhat: Funding follows momentum. Sell a quick, documented, working first slice to keep the initiative alive past the first review.

cards.prompt: Our logistics scheduling still runs on a mainframe batch that can't do anything real-time.
cards.rootCause: Batch-era architecture can't meet modern real-time needs.
cards.consequence: The business is constrained to overnight cycles.
cards.diagnostic: Which decisions are you forced to make on yesterday's data because of the batch cycle?
cards.angle: Agents document the batch logic to open a path toward real-time processing.
cards.objection: The whole system assumes batch; real-time is impossible.
cards.reframe: It assumes batch because it was built that way — understanding the logic is the first step to selectively moving the parts that need to be real-time.

personaShift.CTO: opens a path to real-time capability

cards.soWhat: Batch limits are business limits. Sell documenting the batch logic as the first concrete step toward real-time.

cards.prompt: We outsourced mainframe maintenance years ago and now we've lost all internal understanding.
cards.rootCause: Years of outsourcing drained internal understanding of the core.
cards.consequence: Total dependence on a vendor for a critical system.
cards.diagnostic: If your maintenance vendor doubled their price tomorrow, what's your alternative?
cards.angle: Agents rebuild internal understanding through documentation, restoring your leverage.
cards.objection: We don't have the people to take it back in-house.
cards.reframe: You don't need a big team — the agent documents the system so a small internal team, plus agent support, can supervise the work and restore your negotiating position.
cards.soWhat: Outsourcing hollowed out your leverage. Sell rebuilding internal understanding through documentation you control.

cards.prompt: Our risk committee says an unproven approach on the core is itself an unacceptable risk.
cards.rootCause: Anything unproven on the core is treated as inherently too risky.
cards.consequence: The safest-looking choice is inaction, which carries its own growing risk.
cards.diagnostic: How does the committee weigh the risk of doing nothing as the expert pool shrinks?
cards.angle: A bounded, read-only-first pilot has near-zero downside and directly de-risks the larger, unmanaged status-quo risk.
cards.objection: An unproven approach on our core is unacceptable.
cards.reframe: Agreed on unproven at scale — so we don't. A read-only, bounded pilot changes nothing and proves the approach. The unacceptable risk is the status quo of losing the last experts.

personaShift.CRO: minimizes downside with a bounded pilot
personaShift.VPE: keeps the core untouched during proof

cards.soWhat: Flip the risk frame: a read-only pilot has near-zero downside; the real risk is the unmanaged status quo.

cards.prompt: We've documented the mainframe twice before and the docs were stale within a year.
cards.rootCause: Manual docs drift out of sync with the code almost immediately.
cards.consequence: Documentation investments keep evaporating.
cards.diagnostic: What made the last documentation effort go stale — the code changed, or nobody maintained the docs?
cards.angle: Agents can regenerate documentation from the current code, keeping it in sync rather than letting it decay.
cards.objection: Documentation always goes stale; why pay for it again?
cards.reframe: It goes stale when it's a one-time manual artifact. Agent-generated docs can be refreshed from the code as it changes, so they stay current instead of decaying.
cards.soWhat: Docs rot the moment they're written. Sell regeneration from current code, not another document that decays.

cards.prompt: Our mainframe's error handling is a maze of GO TOs and nobody can trace a failure path.
cards.rootCause: Spaghetti control flow makes failure paths impossible to follow.
cards.consequence: Incidents take far too long to diagnose.
cards.diagnostic: How long does it typically take to trace a production failure back to its cause in that code?
cards.angle: Agents trace and document the control flow, turning failure diagnosis from hours into minutes.
cards.objection: That control flow is genuinely unfollowable.
cards.reframe: For a human reading top to bottom, yes — an agent follows every branch and GO TO mechanically and produces the flow map you can't build by hand.
cards.soWhat: Slow diagnosis is untraced control flow. Sell the trace that turns hours of debugging into minutes.

cards.prompt: Our manufacturing MRP runs on a mainframe so old the hardware itself is hard to source.
cards.rootCause: Software is welded to hardware that's increasingly impossible to replace.
cards.consequence: A hardware failure could halt production with no easy fix.
cards.diagnostic: What's your plan the day a hardware component fails and can't be sourced?
cards.angle: Agents document the system so it can be moved off the dying hardware in time.
cards.objection: Re-platforming that system is a massive undertaking.
cards.reframe: It is if you start blind. Documenting it first, with agents, makes the re-platform tractable and gives you a head start before the hardware forces your hand.

personaShift.CTO: enables a move off dying hardware

cards.soWhat: Dying hardware is a hard deadline nobody sets on purpose. Sell documenting the system so it can move off in time.

cards.prompt: We need to expose real-time account data from the mainframe for a new mobile app.
cards.rootCause: The core wasn't designed to serve real-time queries to modern channels.
cards.consequence: New customer-facing products stall on core access.
cards.diagnostic: What's the gap between what the mobile app needs and what the core can serve today?
cards.angle: Agents document the access paths and help build a real-time layer over the core.
cards.objection: The core can't handle real-time query load.
cards.reframe: Then the answer is a documented access layer that serves modern channels without hammering the core directly — which starts with understanding what's there.

personaShift.CTO: enables modern channels over the core
personaShift.CRO: controls how the core is accessed

cards.soWhat: New products stall on batch access. Sell documenting the paths that let you build a real-time layer over the core.

cards.prompt: Our CIO was burned by a modernization vendor and now blocks anything touching the mainframe.
cards.rootCause: A prior failure hardened into a blanket veto.
cards.consequence: Even low-risk, high-value work can't get past the gate.
cards.diagnostic: What would the CIO need to see to distinguish this from what burned them before?
cards.angle: A bounded, verifiable pilot with a clear success metric gives the CIO evidence, not another promise.
cards.objection: Our CIO won't let anyone near the mainframe after last time.
cards.reframe: That caution is warranted — so we earn past it with a small, reversible pilot the CIO defines the success metric for. Evidence, not a pitch, is what moves a burned executive.

personaShift.CTO: earns trust through a bounded proof
personaShift.VPE: keeps the core safe during proof

cards.soWhat: A burned CIO wants evidence, not promises. Sell a bounded pilot with one clear success metric they pick.

cards.prompt: We're still on Angular 9. We know we're behind but there's never time.
cards.rootCause: Upgrades lose to feature work every planning cycle.
cards.consequence: Security vulnerabilities accumulate in old dependencies; can't attract engineers to an old stack.
cards.diagnostic: How many known vulnerabilities are sitting in your dependency tree right now because of the version gap?
cards.angle: The work is mechanical, verifiable, and parallelizable — a fleet of agents upgrades every repo at once while engineers approve.
cards.objection: Upgrades always break things in ways we can't predict.
cards.reframe: That's exactly why the agent runs the build and tests after each step and fixes its own regressions — you see a green build and a reviewable PR, not a surprise in production.

personaShift.VPE: clears a backlog nobody wants to own

cards.soWhat: Upgrades are mechanical, verifiable, and parallel — sell a fleet doing every repo at once while engineers just approve.

cards.prompt: Our Java estate is a mix of 8, 11, and 17 depending on which team built it.
cards.rootCause: No central modernization mandate; each team drifted independently.
cards.consequence: Inconsistent security posture, high maintenance burden, onboarding friction.
cards.diagnostic: Do you have a full inventory of what's running which version, or would you have to go find out?
cards.angle: Agents inventory the whole estate and standardize it in parallel.
cards.objection: Standardizing across all those teams is a political nightmare.
cards.reframe: The agent removes the labor argument — teams resist because it's painful manual work. When the upgrade is done for them and just needs review, the politics soften.

personaShift.VPE: clears an upgrade backlog nobody owns

cards.soWhat: You can't standardize what you haven't counted. Sell the estate-wide inventory before the fix.

cards.prompt: Half our services are on Python 2 still. Migrating them keeps slipping.
cards.rootCause: Python 2 is end-of-life; migration is unglamorous and deprioritized.
cards.consequence: Unsupported runtime, security risk, library incompatibility.
cards.diagnostic: What's blocking the Python 2 work from ever reaching the top of a sprint?
cards.angle: Agents do the repetitive 2-to-3 translation and test the result, making a stalled migration finally movable.
cards.objection: Our Python 2 code has too many quirks for automated migration.
cards.reframe: The quirks are exactly where self-correction earns its place — the agent hits the edge case, reads the failure, and fixes it, rather than silently producing broken code.

personaShift.VPE: frees the team from dead-end maintenance

cards.soWhat: A stalled runtime migration moves the moment translation is automated and tested — sell removing the manual bottleneck.

cards.prompt: We tried a dependency-bump tool. It opened 400 PRs nobody had time to review.
cards.rootCause: Naive automation created review load instead of removing it.
cards.consequence: Tool fatigue; skepticism toward any modernization automation.
cards.diagnostic: When that tool opened those PRs, did any of them actually build and pass tests?
cards.angle: The difference is verification — an agent that runs the build, fixes regressions, and only surfaces a green, reviewable PR reduces review load rather than dumping it.
cards.objection: We tried automation and it just made more work.
cards.reframe: That tool bumped versions and walked away. An agent completes the loop — it validates its own work, so you review finished, passing changes, not 400 broken proposals.
cards.soWhat: The difference between dumping work and reducing it is verification — sell green, reviewable PRs, not a pile of diffs.

cards.prompt: Our front-end framework is two major versions from end-of-life and the vendor's dropping support.
cards.rootCause: Framework approaching unsupported status; upgrade deferred too long.
cards.consequence: Imminent loss of support, security patches, and ecosystem compatibility.
cards.diagnostic: What breaks in your ability to ship the day that framework goes unsupported?
cards.angle: Agents drive the version-by-version upgrade to a supported release, verifying at each step.
cards.objection: We'll just do it ourselves when we have to.
cards.reframe: The 'when we have to' moment is usually a crunch under deadline. Doing it now with agent capacity turns a future fire drill into a controlled, low-risk upgrade.
cards.soWhat: EOL isn't a someday problem. Sell the version-by-version climb to a supported release, verified at each step.

cards.prompt: Every time we try to upgrade, one obscure dependency conflict blocks the whole thing.
cards.rootCause: A single incompatible library stalls the entire upgrade path.
cards.consequence: Upgrades abandoned at the first hard conflict; permanent lag.
cards.diagnostic: When the upgrade stalls, is it the volume of changes or one specific blocker that stops you?
cards.angle: Agents diagnose the root conflict and engineer a workaround — this is the Ivy/ng2-charts moment from a real Devin upgrade.
cards.objection: These conflicts need deep expertise to untangle.
cards.reframe: They do — and that's exactly what the agent demonstrates: it hits the conflict, diagnoses the root cause, and engineers a specific workaround, rather than just failing like a find-and-replace tool would.
cards.soWhat: Dependency conflicts are where upgrades die. Sell diagnosing the root conflict, not just bumping numbers.

cards.prompt: Our whole front end is still AngularJS 1.x and Google ended support years ago.
cards.rootCause: A large app stuck on a framework that no longer receives any updates.
cards.consequence: No security patches, shrinking talent pool, mounting risk.
cards.diagnostic: What's your plan for a security issue in a framework nobody maintains anymore?
cards.angle: Agents do the heavy, mechanical migration off the dead framework while your team reviews.
cards.objection: A full AngularJS rewrite is a project we can never find time for.
cards.reframe: It never gets time because it's huge manual work — which is exactly what agents absorb, turning a years-long rewrite into a reviewable, incremental migration.

personaShift.VPE: clears a migration nobody wants to own
personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of maintaining a dead framework

cards.soWhat: A dead framework in production is silent risk. Sell doing the heavy mechanical migration while the team reviews.

cards.prompt: We're several major versions behind on .NET Framework and can't use any modern libraries.
cards.rootCause: Legacy .NET Framework blocks adoption of the modern .NET ecosystem.
cards.consequence: Cut off from current libraries, performance, and hiring appeal.
cards.diagnostic: How often does a library you want turn out to require modern .NET you can't run?
cards.angle: Agents handle the mechanical migration from .NET Framework to modern .NET across the estate.
cards.objection: Our Framework code uses APIs that don't exist in modern .NET.
cards.reframe: Those API gaps are exactly what the agent identifies and rewrites, testing each change — the tricky parts are where it adds the most value.

personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of an isolated stack

cards.soWhat: Lock-in feels permanent until the migration is cheap. Sell moving off .NET Framework across the whole estate at once.

cards.prompt: Our React apps are all old class components and every new hire wants hooks.
cards.rootCause: Large codebase predates modern React patterns.
cards.consequence: Friction hiring and onboarding; diverging from ecosystem norms.
cards.diagnostic: How much of your onboarding is spent explaining patterns the rest of the industry moved past?
cards.angle: Agents mechanically convert class components to modern patterns across the codebase.
cards.objection: Rewriting all those components by hand would take forever.
cards.reframe: By hand, yes — the conversion is repetitive and rule-based, which is why an agent can do it at scale while your team reviews the diffs.

personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of a divergent codebase

cards.soWhat: Legacy patterns spread by copy-paste. Sell mechanically converting them everywhere, not one component at a time by hand.

cards.prompt: We're on Vue 2 and it's officially end-of-life but Vue 3 is a big jump.
cards.rootCause: A breaking major version makes the upgrade feel like a rewrite.
cards.consequence: Stuck on an EOL version rather than face the migration.
cards.diagnostic: What's the cost of staying on an end-of-life framework versus the one-time migration?
cards.angle: Agents work through the breaking changes systematically, component by component, verifying as they go.
cards.objection: Vue 2 to 3 has too many breaking changes to risk.
cards.reframe: The breaking changes are well-defined and repetitive — ideal for an agent that applies them consistently and runs the tests, rather than a risky manual sweep.
cards.soWhat: A major-version cliff is climbed component by component. Sell systematic, verified steps over a scary all-at-once jump.

cards.prompt: Half our pages still depend on jQuery and a pile of plugins from 2014.
cards.rootCause: Old jQuery and unmaintained plugins woven through the UI.
cards.consequence: Fragile, hard-to-secure front end that repels modern developers.
cards.diagnostic: How many of those jQuery plugins are still maintained by anyone?
cards.angle: Agents replace jQuery patterns with modern equivalents incrementally and safely.
cards.objection: That jQuery is load-bearing; ripping it out will break things.
cards.reframe: That's why it's incremental and test-verified — the agent replaces one pattern at a time and confirms nothing breaks before moving on.
cards.soWhat: jQuery dependence is a slow leak. Sell replacing the patterns incrementally so nothing breaks on the way out.

cards.prompt: Upgrades always lose to feature work and it's been that way for three years.
cards.rootCause: Upgrade work has no visible business value, so it never wins planning.
cards.consequence: The version gap widens every cycle.
cards.diagnostic: How many planning cycles in a row has the upgrade been bumped for features?
cards.angle: Agents do the upgrade work without consuming the feature-team capacity it always loses to.
cards.objection: We just need to prioritize upgrades better.
cards.reframe: Better prioritization has failed for three years because it's a capacity fight upgrades can't win. Agents add capacity, so it stops being a fight.
cards.soWhat: Upgrades always lose to features because they share the same team. Sell capacity that doesn't compete with the roadmap.

cards.prompt: Our monorepo has a hundred packages all pinned to different, old dependency versions.
cards.rootCause: No coordinated upgrade path; each package drifted independently.
cards.consequence: Inconsistent behavior, duplicated vulnerabilities, painful shared changes.
cards.diagnostic: Do you even have a single view of which packages are on which versions?
cards.angle: Agents inventory and align dependency versions across the whole monorepo in parallel.
cards.objection: Aligning versions across a hundred packages will break half of them.
cards.reframe: Which is why each package's build and tests run after its bump and regressions are fixed before the PR — you review green changes, not breakage.
cards.soWhat: Version sprawl is an alignment problem, not a rewrite. Sell aligning the whole monorepo in parallel.

cards.prompt: The reason we can't upgrade is a fork of a library we patched years ago and never upstreamed.
cards.rootCause: A local fork diverged from upstream and now blocks every upgrade.
cards.consequence: Permanently pinned to an old version to keep the fork working.
cards.diagnostic: What did you fork that library to fix, and does upstream solve it now?
cards.angle: Agents analyze the fork's changes and reconcile them with current upstream so you can drop the fork.
cards.objection: Untangling that fork from upstream is a nightmare nobody wants.
cards.reframe: The agent diffs your fork against upstream, identifies what's still needed, and reconciles it — the tedious reconciliation is exactly its strength.

personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of maintaining a fork

cards.soWhat: An internal fork is a tax you forgot you're paying. Sell reconciling with upstream so you can finally drop it.

cards.prompt: Our Spring Boot version is so old that the migration guide itself references dead links.
cards.rootCause: The gap is so large that even the upgrade path is poorly documented now.
cards.consequence: Upgrading requires reconstructing a path across multiple missing steps.
cards.diagnostic: How many major versions behind are you, and does a clean upgrade path even still exist?
cards.angle: Agents step through multiple version jumps sequentially, resolving each one's breaking changes.
cards.objection: We're too far behind for any upgrade guide to help.
cards.reframe: That's why a step-by-step agent-driven upgrade works where a guide doesn't — it resolves each intermediate version's issues in sequence rather than assuming one clean leap.

personaShift.CFO: reduces risk of an unsupported stack

cards.soWhat: Extreme lag is many jumps, each with its own breaking changes. Sell stepping through them sequentially, verified.

cards.prompt: Every team says their code is upgrade-ready but nobody's actually verified it.
cards.rootCause: Assumed readiness with no test coverage to confirm it.
cards.consequence: Upgrades stall on surprises that thin tests didn't catch.
cards.diagnostic: If you upgraded tomorrow, how much would your test suite actually catch?
cards.angle: Agents add the missing coverage first, then upgrade against a real safety net.
cards.objection: Adding tests before upgrading doubles the work.
cards.reframe: It's the work that makes the upgrade safe rather than a gamble — and the agent writes the coverage, so it doesn't double your team's load.

personaShift.VPE: upgrades with a real safety net

cards.soWhat: Never upgrade without a net. Sell adding the missing coverage first, then upgrading against real tests.

cards.prompt: Our board keeps asking why upgrades take a quarter each when competitors ship weekly.
cards.rootCause: Manual, serial upgrade work can't keep pace with the ecosystem.
cards.consequence: Falling behind competitors who stay current cheaply.
cards.diagnostic: What would staying continuously current be worth versus these quarterly scrambles?
cards.angle: Agents keep dependencies continuously current in the background instead of in painful batches.
cards.objection: Continuous upgrading sounds like continuous breakage.
cards.reframe: Small, verified, frequent upgrades are far safer than rare big-bang ones — the agent handles each incrementally with tests, so current becomes the low-risk state.

personaShift.CTO: closes the velocity gap with competitors
personaShift.VPE: trades painful batches for steady flow
personaShift.CFO: reduces the cost of falling behind

cards.soWhat: Painful batches happen because currency isn't continuous. Sell keeping dependencies current in the background instead.

cards.prompt: We're on Java 8 and every new hire is surprised we're not on a modern LTS.
cards.rootCause: An old runtime signals a stagnant stack to candidates.
cards.consequence: Harder recruiting and quiet attrition of ambitious engineers.
cards.diagnostic: How often does your stack come up as a concern in candidate conversations?
cards.angle: Agents migrate the estate to a modern LTS, removing a real recruiting handicap.
cards.objection: Our Java 8 code relies on behaviors that changed in later versions.
cards.reframe: Those behavioral changes are known and finite — the agent finds each affected spot, applies the fix, and verifies it, which is safer than a manual audit.

personaShift.CTO: makes the stack attractive to talent

cards.soWhat: A stale stack is a recruiting handicap you can measure in offers declined. Sell the move to a modern LTS.

cards.prompt: We want to adopt a modern build system but we're buried in old Webpack config nobody understands.
cards.rootCause: Years of accreted, undocumented build configuration.
cards.consequence: Slow builds and inability to adopt faster modern tooling.
cards.diagnostic: Who on your team could confidently explain your current build config end to end?
cards.angle: Agents document the existing config and migrate it to modern tooling incrementally.
cards.objection: Our build config is too customized to migrate cleanly.
cards.reframe: Customization is why manual migration stalls — the agent maps every customization and reproduces it on modern tooling, verifying builds along the way.
cards.soWhat: Opaque build tooling blocks everything downstream. Sell documenting the config, then migrating it incrementally.

cards.prompt: Our mobile app is stuck on an old SDK and the app stores are threatening to delist it.
cards.rootCause: App-store policy forces an SDK upgrade the team keeps deferring.
cards.consequence: Risk of delisting — a direct revenue and reputation hit.
cards.diagnostic: What's the store's deadline, and what happens to revenue if you miss it?
cards.angle: Agents drive the SDK migration to hit the store deadline with the deprecated APIs handled.
cards.objection: The new SDK deprecates APIs our whole app is built on.
cards.reframe: Each deprecated API has a migration path — the agent applies them systematically across the app and tests the result, which is how you hit the deadline reliably.
cards.soWhat: A store deadline is leverage, not a threat. Sell driving the SDK migration to hit it with deprecated APIs handled.

cards.prompt: We keep patching around an old ORM version because upgrading means touching every query.
cards.rootCause: A deeply embedded dependency makes upgrades touch the whole codebase.
cards.consequence: The upgrade feels too broad to ever start.
cards.diagnostic: How many places would an ORM upgrade actually touch — and is that knowable today?
cards.angle: Agents handle broad, repetitive, cross-cutting changes exactly like this at scale.
cards.objection: An upgrade that touches every query is too risky.
cards.reframe: Broad but repetitive is the ideal agent task — it applies the change everywhere consistently and the test suite catches regressions, which manual editing can't guarantee.
cards.soWhat: Broad, repetitive, cross-cutting changes are exactly what agents scale — sell the pervasive-dependency upgrade at once.

cards.prompt: We tried Dependabot and it just opened hundreds of PRs that broke the build.
cards.rootCause: A bump tool created review load and breakage instead of finished work.
cards.consequence: Distrust of any dependency automation.
cards.diagnostic: Of those PRs, how many actually built and passed tests on their own?
cards.angle: An agent runs the build, fixes the regressions, and only surfaces green, reviewable PRs.
cards.objection: We tried dependency automation and it made things worse.
cards.reframe: A bump-only tool walks away after changing versions. An agent completes the loop — it fixes what its own upgrade broke, so you review passing changes, not a pile of red builds.
cards.soWhat: Backlash comes from naive automation. Sell the agent that builds, fixes regressions, and surfaces only green PRs.

cards.prompt: Our team says upgrades never deliver visible value, so leadership won't fund them.
cards.rootCause: Upgrade benefits are risk-avoidance and don't show on a roadmap.
cards.consequence: Upgrades are perpetually unfunded until something breaks.
cards.diagnostic: What's the cost the day an unpatched dependency causes an incident or a failed audit?
cards.angle: Frame upgrades as cheap continuous insurance the agent makes nearly free, versus expensive crisis remediation.
cards.objection: Upgrades don't deliver anything the business can see.
cards.reframe: They deliver until they don't — the value is the incident, breach, or failed audit you avoid. Agents make that insurance so cheap it's no longer a hard trade-off.
cards.soWhat: Frame upgrades as cheap continuous insurance the agent makes near-free — versus expensive crisis remediation later.

cards.prompt: Our senior devs insist upgrades need their hands-on attention and can't be delegated.
cards.rootCause: Belief that only senior judgment can safely perform upgrades.
cards.consequence: Upgrades bottleneck on the busiest, most expensive people.
cards.diagnostic: How much of an upgrade is genuinely judgment versus repetitive mechanical change?
cards.angle: Seniors direct and review; the agent does the mechanical bulk — their judgment stays, the bottleneck goes.
cards.objection: Upgrades are too important to hand off to automation.
cards.reframe: The judgment stays with your seniors — they review every change. What's delegated is the repetitive editing that was wasting their time, not the decisions.

personaShift.CTO: applies senior judgment as review, not grind
personaShift.CFO: frees expensive people from mechanical work
personaShift.CRO: keeps expert oversight on every change

cards.soWhat: Delegation resistance melts when seniors keep the judgment. Sell them directing while the agent does the bulk.

cards.prompt: We've been burned by an upgrade that silently broke production months later.
cards.rootCause: A past upgrade introduced a subtle bug that surfaced too late.
cards.consequence: Deep caution toward any upgrade at all.
cards.diagnostic: Did that upgrade have test coverage that could have caught it before release?
cards.angle: Agents add coverage around the change and verify behavior, catching the subtle regressions manual upgrades miss.
cards.objection: Upgrades introduce bugs we don't find until it's too late.
cards.reframe: That happens when upgrades outrun the tests. The agent strengthens coverage as part of the work, so subtle regressions surface in CI, not months later in production.
cards.soWhat: Manual upgrades miss the subtle regressions. Sell coverage around the change that catches what eyes don't.

cards.prompt: Our Rails app is two major versions behind and gems we depend on are abandoned.
cards.rootCause: Old framework version pins you to gems no longer maintained.
cards.consequence: Unmaintained dependencies with unpatched issues.
cards.diagnostic: How many of your gems have had zero commits in the last two years?
cards.angle: Agents upgrade the framework and replace abandoned gems with maintained equivalents.
cards.objection: Swapping out abandoned gems could change behavior subtly.
cards.reframe: The agent replaces them behind your test suite, verifying behavior matches — subtle changes get caught, not shipped.
cards.soWhat: Abandoned dependencies don't get safer with time. Sell upgrading the framework and swapping in maintained equivalents.

cards.prompt: Our Django is old and half our middleware relies on behavior that later versions changed.
cards.rootCause: Code depends on quirks that newer framework versions altered.
cards.consequence: Upgrading risks silent behavioral changes across the app.
cards.diagnostic: Do you actually know which behaviors your code depends on that changed upstream?
cards.angle: Agents identify every dependence on changed behavior and adapt the code, verified by tests.
cards.objection: We can't even enumerate what behavior we depend on.
cards.reframe: The agent enumerates it by analyzing the code against the version deltas — surfacing dependencies you couldn't list by hand and fixing each.
cards.soWhat: Behavior-dependent lag hides landmines. Sell finding every dependence on changed behavior and adapting it under test.

cards.prompt: We standardized on a framework that's now losing community momentum and we're worried.
cards.rootCause: The chosen framework's community and support are shrinking.
cards.consequence: Rising risk of being stranded on a fading platform.
cards.diagnostic: What's your exit path if that framework's community keeps shrinking?
cards.angle: Agents make an eventual migration off the fading framework affordable and incremental.
cards.objection: Migrating off it now feels premature and expensive.
cards.reframe: You don't have to migrate now — but knowing an agent-driven migration is affordable turns a scary lock-in into a managed option you can exercise when ready.
cards.soWhat: Betting on a fading ecosystem compounds. Sell making the eventual migration affordable and incremental now, not later.

cards.prompt: Our front end and back end are on such different versions they barely share tooling anymore.
cards.rootCause: Front and back ends drifted onto incompatible tooling generations.
cards.consequence: Duplicated effort and friction across the stack boundary.
cards.diagnostic: How much tooling and knowledge can your front-end and back-end teams actually share today?
cards.angle: Agents realign both sides toward compatible, current tooling in parallel.
cards.objection: The two stacks are too different to bring back into alignment.
cards.reframe: The agent works both sides simultaneously, converging them on shared, current tooling — the parallel scale is what makes realignment feasible.
cards.soWhat: Version divergence breaks at the seams. Sell realigning both sides toward compatible, current tooling in parallel.

cards.prompt: We need to adopt a new framework feature but our version is too old to support it.
cards.rootCause: A needed capability requires a version the team hasn't upgraded to.
cards.consequence: Product work is blocked behind an overdue upgrade.
cards.diagnostic: How many product features are currently blocked by your framework version?
cards.angle: Agents perform the upgrade that unblocks the feature work, verified end to end.
cards.objection: We'd rather build the feature than spend time upgrading.
cards.reframe: The feature can't be built on your version — so the upgrade is the feature's prerequisite. The agent does it fast so your team gets to the feature sooner, not later.
cards.soWhat: When a version blocks a feature, the upgrade is feature work. Sell it as unblocking revenue, not housekeeping.

cards.prompt: We still ship a Bootstrap 3 UI and it looks and behaves a decade out of date.
cards.rootCause: An old CSS/UI framework locks the product's look and behavior in the past.
cards.consequence: Dated UX and incompatibility with modern components.
cards.diagnostic: How much does the dated look affect how customers perceive the product?
cards.angle: Agents migrate the UI framework and update the component patterns incrementally.
cards.objection: A UI framework migration will visually break everything.
cards.reframe: The agent migrates incrementally with visual and test checks, so you catch breakage per section rather than all at once.
cards.soWhat: A stale UI framework ages the whole product. Sell migrating it and modernizing the component patterns incrementally.

cards.prompt: Our Node services are past end-of-life LTS and our hosting provider is dropping support.
cards.rootCause: An end-of-life runtime is now also losing platform support.
cards.consequence: Forced upgrade under an external deadline.
cards.diagnostic: When does your provider stop supporting that runtime, and what's your migration state?
cards.angle: Agents upgrade the runtime and fix incompatibilities across services before the provider cutoff.
cards.objection: Upgrading all our Node services at once is too big before that deadline.
cards.reframe: Agents work the services in parallel and fix incompatibilities per service — the parallelism is what makes the deadline achievable.
cards.soWhat: A provider cutoff is a wall with a date on it. Sell upgrading the runtime and fixing incompatibilities before it hits.

cards.prompt: Finance says we already paid for this framework, so why spend again to change it.
cards.rootCause: Past investment is treated as a reason to keep an aging framework.
cards.consequence: Compounding maintenance and risk cost to protect a sunk investment.
cards.diagnostic: What's the ongoing cost of staying, compared to the one-time cost of moving?
cards.angle: Agents make the move cheap enough that ongoing lag cost clearly outweighs it.
cards.objection: We already invested in this framework; changing it wastes that.
cards.reframe: The past spend is gone either way. The real comparison is future cost — and staying on a lagging framework keeps costing in maintenance and risk, which an affordable agent-led move ends.

personaShift.CFO: compares forward cost, not sunk cost
personaShift.CTO: frees the roadmap from a legacy bet
personaShift.CRO: reduces risk of an aging framework

cards.soWhat: Sunk cost keeps teams stuck. Sell making the move cheap enough that ongoing lag cost clearly outweighs it.

cards.prompt: Our design system is built on a component library version that's now three majors behind.
cards.rootCause: The base library of your design system is far behind current.
cards.consequence: Every product built on it inherits the lag.
cards.diagnostic: How many products would a design-system library upgrade touch downstream?
cards.angle: Agents upgrade the foundation library and propagate the changes to every consuming product.
cards.objection: Upgrading the foundation would ripple into every product at once.
cards.reframe: The ripple is exactly why agents fit — they propagate and verify the change across every consumer in parallel rather than leaving teams to each scramble.
cards.soWhat: A lagging foundation library holds every consumer hostage. Sell upgrading it and propagating to all of them.

cards.prompt: Our GraphQL library is old and the whole API layer is coupled to its quirks.
cards.rootCause: The API layer is tightly bound to an outdated library's specifics.
cards.consequence: The API can't evolve without a risky library upgrade.
cards.diagnostic: How much of your API behavior depends on quirks of that specific library version?
cards.angle: Agents decouple and upgrade the library while preserving API behavior under test.
cards.objection: Our API contract could change if we touch that library.
cards.reframe: The agent upgrades behind contract tests, so any change to the API surface is caught before it ships — the contract is protected, not gambled.

personaShift.CTO: lets the API layer evolve again
personaShift.VPE: removes a version constraint on the API
personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of a frozen API

cards.soWhat: API coupling makes upgrades scary. Sell decoupling and upgrading while preserving behavior under test.

cards.prompt: Our PHP version is end-of-life and our CMS plugins haven't been updated in years.
cards.rootCause: An end-of-life language runtime paired with unmaintained plugins.
cards.consequence: Unsupported runtime and a wide, stale attack surface.
cards.diagnostic: What's exposed to the internet on that end-of-life PHP right now?
cards.angle: Agents upgrade the PHP runtime and modernize or replace the stale plugins.
cards.objection: Our custom plugins won't survive a PHP upgrade.
cards.reframe: The agent updates the custom plugin code to the new runtime and verifies it — the plugins get carried forward, not abandoned.
cards.soWhat: An EOL language drags stale plugins with it. Sell upgrading the runtime and modernizing or replacing the plugins.

cards.prompt: We want to move to a modern testing framework but everything's tied to a deprecated one.
cards.rootCause: The test suite is built on a deprecated framework blocking modern tooling.
cards.consequence: Tests rot and modern testing patterns stay out of reach.
cards.diagnostic: How much does your deprecated test tooling slow down or discourage writing tests?
cards.angle: Agents migrate the test suite to modern tooling, converting the existing tests as they go.
cards.objection: Rewriting the whole test suite is a huge distraction.
cards.reframe: The conversion is mechanical and repetitive — the agent migrates the tests while your team keeps shipping, rather than pausing delivery for a rewrite.

personaShift.CFO: reduces cost of poor test tooling

cards.soWhat: Deprecated test tooling quietly rots your safety net. Sell migrating the suite and converting the tests as you go.

cards.prompt: Every service uses a slightly different, old version of our shared internal library.
cards.rootCause: No coordinated rollout, so each service pinned a different old version.
cards.consequence: Bug fixes and features don't propagate; support is a nightmare.
cards.diagnostic: When you fix a bug in the shared library, how long until every service actually has it?
cards.angle: Agents roll the current shared-library version across all services and fix call-site breakage.
cards.objection: Forcing every service onto the current version will break call sites.
cards.reframe: The agent updates each call site and verifies with tests, so the rollout lands as green PRs rather than broken services.
cards.soWhat: Fragmented internal libraries multiply maintenance. Sell rolling the current version across services and fixing call sites.

cards.prompt: Our architects worry automated upgrades will erode the deliberate choices they made.
cards.rootCause: Fear that mechanical upgrades will overwrite intentional design decisions.
cards.consequence: Architects block automation to protect design integrity.
cards.diagnostic: Which design decisions must be preserved through any upgrade — and are they written down?
cards.angle: Architects define the constraints; the agent upgrades within them and surfaces anything that conflicts for review.
cards.objection: Automated upgrades will trample our architectural decisions.
cards.reframe: The agent works within the constraints your architects set and flags conflicts rather than silently overriding them — design intent stays under human control.

personaShift.CRO: keeps human control over key decisions

cards.soWhat: Architects fear losing intent. Sell the agent upgrading within their constraints and flagging conflicts for review.

cards.prompt: We've frozen dependencies entirely because we're scared of what an upgrade might break.
cards.rootCause: Fear of breakage led to freezing everything, accreting risk.
cards.consequence: A growing wall of debt and vulnerabilities behind the freeze.
cards.diagnostic: How large has the gap grown since you froze — and how much scarier does that make eventually unfreezing?
cards.angle: Agents unfreeze safely by upgrading incrementally with tests, so the wall never has to be scaled all at once.
cards.objection: Unfreezing now would unleash all the breakage we've been avoiding.
cards.reframe: That's why it's done incrementally and verified — the agent chips the wall down one safe step at a time rather than one terrifying leap.

personaShift.CFO: stops debt compounding behind the freeze
