cards.rootCause: Treating an agent as a discrete tool misses that value compounds as teams commit and expand usage.
cards.consequence: Measuring it like a point tool understates the impact and slows the commitment.
cards.diagnostic: What happens to throughput when one squad fully commits, versus dabbling across many?
cards.angle: Stop A/B dabbling; measure what happens when a squad commits fully, then scale the committed model org-wide.
cards.objection: Without a clean A/B test, how do we justify going all-in?
cards.reframe: A/B assumes a fixed tool applied to fixed tasks. In reality teams keep discovering new ways to use it — so measure a fully-committed squad's output. That's the signal that justifies the rollout.
cards.proofMetric: One committed squad delivered 2 releases in 3 months (double the plan); adoption grew to 75% of teams with a ~1,000-developer waitlist

personaShift.CTO: measures the committed-team model, not a muddy A/B
personaShift.VPE: sees compounding gains as teams expand usage organically
personaShift.CFO: justifies the investment on a fully-committed squad's output
personaShift.CRO: reads real demand from the internal waitlist

cards.soWhat: Don't A/B a compounding capability. Measure a fully-committed squad — that's the signal that justifies going all-in.

cards.prompt: If we roll this out top-down, how do we make sure engineers actually get good at delegating to it instead of resenting it?
cards.rootCause: Adoption requires engineers to shift from executors to coordinators, a skill that must be taught.
cards.consequence: Without deliberate enablement, a mandate breeds resentment and shallow usage.
cards.diagnostic: How would you measure whether an engineer has actually learned to delegate well, so you can coach the rest?
cards.angle: Make delegation a tracked, teachable skill — score specificity, back-and-forth, and codebase impact — so enablement is systematic, not hopeful.
cards.objection: You can't teach a whole org to change how it works overnight.
cards.reframe: You teach it the way you teach any skill: make it measurable. Score how well engineers delegate, coach from the data, and the mandate becomes capability instead of compliance.
cards.proofMetric: FE fundinfo's engineer scoring system drove a 10% immediate capacity gain and a projected 2–4x over 2–5 years

personaShift.CTO: turns a mandate into a durable, taught capability
personaShift.VPE: coaches delegation skill from real data, not vibes
personaShift.CFO: protects the rollout investment with measured skill growth
personaShift.CRO: shows buyers a concrete enablement path, de-risking the deal

cards.soWhat: Make delegation measurable and you can coach it. A mandate becomes capability instead of compliance.

cards.prompt: My QE staff are terrified this replaces them. How do I mandate adoption without gutting morale?
cards.rootCause: Fear that automation eliminates roles rather than elevating them.
cards.consequence: A threatened team quietly resists, and the mandate dies from the bottom up.
cards.diagnostic: What higher-value roles could your QE staff grow into once the rote testing is automated?
cards.angle: Position the mandate as a career upgrade — QE staff become agent builders, SREs, and DevOps specialists — and create a role that champions the transition.
cards.objection: Isn't this just a polite way of automating my team out of a job?
cards.reframe: The opposite. The rote work goes to agents; your people move up into agent-builder, SRE, and DevOps roles. Name a champion role to lead it, and the team reinvents its careers instead of defending the status quo.
cards.proofMetric: At Litera a QE individual contributor rose to acting Director of Quality by leading the agent rollout; new 'AI composer' roles emerged

personaShift.CTO: elevates the team's charter instead of shrinking it
personaShift.VPE: converts fear into an upskilling and retention story
personaShift.CFO: redeploys talent to higher-value work, not layoffs
personaShift.CRO: gives the mandate a morale-positive narrative that sticks

cards.soWhat: Fear kills mandates from the bottom. Sell the career upgrade — rote work to agents, people into higher-value roles.

cards.prompt: Our biggest hidden cost is that only a few senior engineers understand our sprawling legacy systems, and onboarding anyone new takes forever.
cards.rootCause: Institutional knowledge is trapped in a few heads across a massive codebase, throttling everyone else.
cards.consequence: Onboarding drags, senior engineers are constant bottlenecks, and change is slow and risky.
cards.diagnostic: What would change if any engineer could query your architecture like a tenured senior engineer, on demand?
cards.angle: Deploy an always-current knowledge layer (auto-generated docs + search) as the enablement wedge, so the mandate makes the whole org faster, not just power users.
cards.objection: Documentation always goes stale the moment it's written.
cards.reframe: This documentation regenerates with every code change and answers questions with diagrams and dependency graphs. It's a living knowledge layer, not a stale wiki — so onboarding and modernization speed up permanently.
cards.proofMetric: Itaú deployed Devin Wiki/Search across a 300,000+-repo codebase; principal engineers now design architecture ~10x faster

personaShift.CTO: builds durable organizational memory across the estate
personaShift.VPE: cuts onboarding and unblocks juniors without senior time
personaShift.CFO: reduces dependence on a few expensive key people
personaShift.CRO: lowers key-person and continuity risk bank-wide

cards.soWhat: Knowledge trapped in a few heads throttles everyone. Sell a living knowledge layer that regenerates with the code.

cards.prompt: We're evaluating every major AI coding tool against real engineering challenges. We set a deliberately high bar: own a task end to end, with a full audit trail.
cards.rootCause: The buyer will only trust a tool that provably completes their hardest real task, not a demo.
cards.consequence: Tools that can't finish the hard task get eliminated no matter how good the pitch.
cards.diagnostic: What's the one real task in your eval that you expect most tools to fail — and what does 'done' look like on it?
cards.angle: Compete on the buyer's own hardest challenge; being the only tool that completes it end to end wins the deal outright.
cards.objection: Every vendor claims they can handle our hardest task.
cards.reframe: So don't take the claim — put it in the bake-off. Give every tool your real challenge with dependencies and no existing logic, and pick whichever actually finishes it. Let the eval decide.
cards.proofMetric: In Evinova's evaluation, Devin was the only tool to complete a complex task with third-party dependencies and no existing logic

personaShift.CTO: selects on proven capability, not marketing
personaShift.VPE: trusts a tool that finished the team's real hard task
personaShift.CFO: buys the winner of an objective, apples-to-apples test
personaShift.CRO: de-risks the decision with an auditable head-to-head

cards.soWhat: Don't argue the claim — win the eval. Being the only tool that finishes the buyer's hardest task closes the deal.

cards.prompt: We're safety-critical automotive. Any tool has to clear enterprise requirements — IDE support, a context engine for huge legacy code, mature security — before we even pilot.
cards.rootCause: Enterprise buyers gate the eval on hard requirements before capability even gets tested.
cards.consequence: Tools that miss the enterprise bar never reach the pilot, regardless of raw ability.
cards.diagnostic: Which enterprise requirements are non-negotiable gates for you, and which tools clear all of them at once?
cards.angle: Meet the full enterprise bar first, then split the work — interactive assistance for one need, autonomous background work for another — so each tool is used where it's strongest.
cards.objection: We'd rather standardize on a single AI tool than juggle several.
cards.reframe: Match the tool to the job: interactive coding assistance and autonomous background execution are different needs. Using the right one for each — both clearing your enterprise bar — beats forcing one tool to do everything.
cards.proofMetric: After a full enterprise evaluation, RV Tech adopted Windsurf for interactive coding and Devin for autonomous work; test-gen velocity rose 10x

personaShift.CTO: satisfies non-negotiable enterprise gates up front
personaShift.VPE: puts the right tool on the right kind of work
personaShift.CFO: avoids paying for a poor single-tool compromise
personaShift.CRO: clears security and compliance requirements before pilot

cards.soWhat: Enterprise gates come before capability. Clear them all, then put the right tool on the right kind of work.

cards.prompt: Sprint velocity swings too much for us to isolate the tool's real impact. Leadership wants a clean signal before we scale.
cards.rootCause: Noisy team-level metrics obscure the true contribution, stalling the scale decision.
cards.consequence: Without a clean signal, the rollout stays stuck in perpetual evaluation.
cards.diagnostic: What unit of output could you measure directly — at the PR level — to cut through the velocity noise?
cards.angle: Measure impact where it's undeniable: merged PRs at your review bar. A clean PR-level signal replaces muddy velocity debates and unlocks the scale decision.
cards.objection: Our metrics are too noisy to prove this is worth expanding.
cards.reframe: Then stop measuring at the sprint level. Track Devin's merged PRs and merge rate directly — production-level output your seniors already reviewed. That's the clean signal leadership is asking for.
cards.proofMetric: By tracking PRs directly, Citation got a clean signal: 271 merged PRs at an 80% rate, plus a 147-PR feature (~367 story points) shipped in weeks

personaShift.CTO: bases the scale decision on a clean, direct signal
personaShift.VPE: cuts through velocity noise to real output
personaShift.CFO: ties spend to merged, reviewed production work
personaShift.CRO: answers 'is it working?' with defensible numbers

cards.soWhat: When velocity is noisy, measure at the PR level. A clean, direct signal unlocks the scale decision.

cards.prompt: Even if it wins the eval, I'm skeptical it'll deliver measurable ROI once it's running day to day.
cards.rootCause: Doubt that eval-stage performance translates into sustained, quantifiable returns.
cards.consequence: Without a running ROI number, the deal stalls at 'nice demo' and never scales.
cards.diagnostic: What recurring, well-bounded workflow could run on a schedule and compound its return over time?
cards.angle: Turn the eval win into a standing workflow — a scheduled playbook that works tickets end to end — and measure the compounding return on setup time.
cards.objection: How do I know the ROI holds up beyond the pilot?
cards.reframe: Instrument it as a recurring workflow, not a one-off. A playbook that runs several times a day and closes tickets end to end shows a return you can watch compound — the ROI is measured, not assumed.
cards.proofMetric: Evinova's 'AutoFixer' playbook attempted 79 bugs in 22 days, merging clean fixes for ~half — a 100% return on under two days of setup

personaShift.CTO: proves durable value, not a one-off eval result
personaShift.VPE: automates a recurring workflow with compounding returns
personaShift.CFO: sees ROI measured on setup cost, not promised
personaShift.CRO: converts an eval win into a defensible business case

cards.soWhat: An eval win isn't ROI. Turn it into a scheduled playbook and let the return compound on a day of setup.

cards.prompt: Our requirements-to-code-to-test traceability relies on brute-force meetings — systems and software engineers going line by line. It's slow and painful.
cards.rootCause: Quality assurance depends on manual, meeting-heavy review that can't keep pace.
cards.consequence: Slow review cycles bottleneck delivery and let requirement conflicts slip through.
cards.diagnostic: If an agent generated your test cases and flagged conflicting requirements, where would your engineers add the most value?
cards.angle: Have the agent ingest the framework, code, and requirements to generate and iterate test cases; engineers shift to verifying the tests check the right logic.
cards.objection: Auto-generated tests won't catch the subtle issues our reviews do.
cards.reframe: They catch more, sooner. In the pilot the agent even flagged conflicting requirements a human confirmed. Engineers stop writing boilerplate tests and start verifying logic across the whole V-model — faster and deeper.
cards.proofMetric: Devin writes ~100% of SIL test code, lifting output from 1–2 tests/day to 10–15; it even caught conflicting requirements needing revision

personaShift.CTO: raises quality coverage across the whole V-model
personaShift.VPE: replaces brute-force review meetings with fast iteration
personaShift.CFO: recovers engineer-hours lost to manual test authoring

cards.soWhat: Replace brute-force review meetings with generated coverage — agents even surface conflicting requirements humans miss.

cards.prompt: Our whole industry is at a turning point — the carriers that can build and adapt software fastest will pull away from the ones stuck on legacy. We can't afford to be the latter.
cards.rootCause: A market-wide shift makes engineering speed the deciding competitive factor, now.
cards.consequence: Firms that move slowly at the inflection point get permanently outrun by faster rivals.
cards.diagnostic: In your market, what becomes possible for the players who can ship and adapt software the fastest?
cards.angle: Sell the inflection: when engineering capacity stops being the constraint, launching a product or entering a market becomes a question of intent, not headcount.
cards.objection: This feels like hype — every vendor says the industry is 'transforming.'
cards.reframe: The evidence is external: analysts estimate agentic AI can cut the slowest, costliest modernization cycles by up to 90%. The question isn't whether the shift happens — it's whether you lead it or get outrun by whoever does.
cards.proofMetric: Hippo partnered with Cognition to embed Devin across engineering, positioning to build and test across all 50 states without multiplying manual effort

personaShift.CTO: reframes capacity as intent, not headcount
personaShift.VPE: makes speed the durable competitive advantage
personaShift.CFO: frames the spend as defending market position
personaShift.CRO: ties the deal to winning an industry inflection

cards.soWhat: Sell the moment, not the feature. When capacity stops being the constraint, launching becomes a question of intent.

cards.prompt: We operate in a heavily regulated environment. Every line of code must be traceable, every change auditable, every release defensible to regulators.
cards.rootCause: Regulation is the very constraint that makes disciplined, auditable agent workflows so valuable now.
cards.consequence: Firms treating compliance as a blocker miss that it's exactly where agents create the most leverage.
cards.diagnostic: Which regulated, well-bounded work — like specifications or audit docs — eats the most senior time you'd rather spend elsewhere?
cards.angle: Sell into the regulatory moment: agents own the structured, auditable work (specs, DR plans, coverage evidence) while preserving the full audit trail regulators demand.
cards.objection: Regulated work is the last place we'd trust an AI.
cards.reframe: It's the best-suited place. The work is bounded and the standard is defined — the agent drafts to ~90% accuracy and your seniors become reviewers, where their regulatory judgment matters most, with the audit trail intact.
cards.proofMetric: Evinova cut GxP documentation from 35–40 hours to under 5 (8x faster) at ~90% first-draft accuracy, preserving the audit trail for 21 CFR Part 11

personaShift.CTO: makes regulated work a source of leverage, not drag
personaShift.VPE: moves seniors from authoring to high-value review
personaShift.CFO: reclaims 30+ senior hours per regulatory document

cards.soWhat: Regulation isn't the blocker — it's where agents create the most leverage. Bounded, defined work with the audit trail intact.

cards.prompt: Software-defined vehicles change everything for us — cars that improve after they leave the factory. But safety-critical delivery is slow and review-heavy.
cards.rootCause: A new product paradigm demands far faster, higher-quality delivery than legacy processes allow.
cards.consequence: Teams that can't accelerate safely miss the window the new paradigm opens.
cards.diagnostic: As you shift to software-defined vehicles, what has to get faster without ever lowering the safety bar?
cards.angle: Sell the paradigm shift: autonomous agents triage issues, generate test coverage, and scan for regressions so engineers focus on the features that differentiate the vehicle.
cards.objection: Moving faster in safety-critical software sounds reckless.
cards.reframe: Speed and safety compound here. Agents add layers of QA — test generation, regression scans, triage — so you catch more, sooner. Engineers spend their time on features, not brute-force review, and quality goes up as velocity does.
cards.proofMetric: RV Tech redirected 3–4 engineers from triage back to product and lifted test-generation velocity 10x, targeting a platform that could reach 30M vehicles

personaShift.CTO: delivers on the software-defined-vehicle promise safely
personaShift.VPE: adds QA layers while accelerating delivery
personaShift.CFO: redirects scarce engineers to differentiating work
personaShift.CRO: improves predictability and quality at the inflection

cards.soWhat: A new product paradigm rewards speed with safety. Agents add QA layers so quality rises as velocity does.

cards.prompt: Most carriers spend the majority of their IT budget just maintaining legacy systems. Why would betting on agents now change our position?
cards.rootCause: The status quo locks budget into maintenance, leaving nothing to capitalize on the shift.
cards.consequence: Staying on legacy widens the gap as faster competitors compound their lead.
cards.diagnostic: If you could redirect the budget currently trapped in legacy maintenance, what new products would you finally build?
cards.angle: Sell the reallocation: embedding agents into the systems that run the business frees maintenance budget for the product and risk work that moves the needle.
cards.objection: Betting on new technology mid-cycle feels riskier than staying put.
cards.reframe: Staying put is the risk — the gap between fast and legacy carriers only widens from here. Owning your core tech and embedding agents lets you build and test across every state without multiplying effort, so intent, not headcount, sets your pace.
cards.proofMetric: Hippo builds and owns its core tech in-house; with Devin it can build and test across all 50 states without multiplying manual effort

personaShift.CTO: frees budget from maintenance for new capability
personaShift.VPE: redirects effort from upkeep to product work
personaShift.CRO: closes the competitive gap instead of widening it

cards.soWhat: Staying on legacy is the risk. Sell reallocating maintenance budget into the products that win the inflection.

cards.prompt: We're private-equity backed and competing hard. Our customers keep asking how we ship so much faster than the rest of the market.
cards.rootCause: Competitive pressure at a market inflection rewards whoever can compound delivery speed the fastest.
cards.consequence: Rivals that don't accelerate lose the innovation-pace narrative customers now judge them on.
cards.diagnostic: How much of your competitive differentiation now comes down to raw pace of innovation?
cards.angle: Sell speed as differentiation: embedding agents lets a mature org launch at startup cadence, and customers notice the pace.
cards.objection: Enterprise-scale companies can't really move at startup speed.
cards.reframe: They can when engineering stops being the constraint. Teams operating like a much larger org launched five products in three weeks — competitors literally called to ask what changed. Pace becomes the brand.
cards.proofMetric: Litera launched 5 major products in 3 weeks ahead of its flagship event; CTO describes '250 engineers operating at the speed of a thousand'

personaShift.CTO: lets an enterprise org move at startup cadence
personaShift.VPE: compounds delivery speed into a durable edge
personaShift.CFO: turns pace into a PE-backed growth lever
personaShift.CRO: makes innovation velocity a customer-facing differentiator

cards.soWhat: Pace becomes the brand. An enterprise that ships at startup cadence makes rivals call to ask what changed.

cards.prompt: We've proven this internally. Now we want to build a repeatable client-facing practice around it — not just use it ourselves.
cards.rootCause: Internal wins stay one-off unless they're packaged into a repeatable, sellable offering.
cards.consequence: Without productizing, hard-won expertise never scales beyond your own four walls.
cards.diagnostic: Which of your proven internal use cases could become a templated service you sell to clients?
cards.angle: Wrap the proven methodology in advisory, enablement, customization, and ROI analytics — a full partner offering with the agent as the keystone.
cards.objection: Reusing our internal approach with clients won't survive contact with their messy environments.
cards.reframe: That's why you sell the methodology, not the environment. Each internal use case becomes a reusable playbook; the practice meets clients where they are — engineers, acceleration, or fully managed — all on the model you proved on yourself.
cards.proofMetric: AHEAD built playbooks for Terraform, data models, Azure, Go, Salesforce/MuleSoft testing — the keystone of its AI-accelerated-development practice

personaShift.CTO: turns proven internal capability into a scalable practice
personaShift.VPE: packages repeatable playbooks the team already validated
personaShift.CFO: creates a new revenue line from existing expertise
personaShift.CRO: sells a productized offering, not bespoke consulting

cards.soWhat: Package proven internal wins into a repeatable offering. Sell the methodology, and meet clients wherever they are.

cards.prompt: Every client engagement feels like starting from scratch. We can't scale a practice if each project reinvents the wheel.
cards.rootCause: Bespoke, non-repeatable delivery caps how many clients a practice can serve.
cards.consequence: Margins and growth stall because each engagement consumes senior time from zero.
cards.diagnostic: Which delivery steps repeat across engagements often enough to template into a playbook?
cards.angle: Convert each proven use case into a reusable playbook so every new engagement onboards faster than the last.
cards.objection: Our clients' problems are too varied to templatize.
cards.reframe: The problems vary; the motions repeat. Terraform upgrades, data-model evolution, test generation — template those as playbooks and each engagement starts from a proven asset, not a blank page.
cards.proofMetric: AHEAD reports every new team onboards faster than the last as its library of reusable Devin playbooks grows

personaShift.CTO: standardizes delivery on proven, reusable assets
personaShift.VPE: cuts ramp time on every new engagement
personaShift.CRO: scales the number of clients a team can serve

cards.soWhat: Bespoke delivery caps a practice. Template the repeating motions into playbooks so each engagement starts ahead.

cards.prompt: Our clients don't just want technical fixes — they're solving business problems and want to see returns their board can act on.
cards.rootCause: Buyers value outcomes and board-level ROI, not tooling for its own sake.
cards.consequence: A practice that sells activity instead of outcomes struggles to justify its price to executives.
cards.diagnostic: What returns would a client's board need to see to treat this as a strategic investment, not a line item?
cards.angle: Bundle ROI analytics into the offering — measure the return in terms a board can act on — and meet clients wherever they are on the adoption curve.
cards.objection: How do we prove business value, not just engineering output?
cards.reframe: Instrument the outcome. Build ROI analytics into the engagement so returns land in board-ready terms; the agent handles the work while your practice reports the business impact clients actually buy.
cards.proofMetric: AHEAD's offering pairs delivery with ROI analytics that measure returns 'in terms a board can act on,' force-multiplying client transformations

personaShift.CTO: connects technical work to strategic outcomes
personaShift.VPE: delivers alongside clients with measurable impact
personaShift.CFO: reports returns in board-ready ROI terms
personaShift.CRO: sells business outcomes, elevating deal value

cards.soWhat: Buyers fund outcomes, not activity. Bundle ROI analytics so returns land in terms a board can act on.

cards.prompt: Some clients want us to run this as an ongoing managed service, but I worry we're just renting out capacity we can't differentiate.
cards.rootCause: A managed service without a proven methodology is undifferentiated body-shopping.
cards.consequence: Commoditized delivery competes only on price and erodes the practice's value.
cards.diagnostic: What proven methodology and playbook library makes your managed service defensible versus a generic staffing shop?
cards.angle: Differentiate the managed service on the methodology you proved on yourself and the playbook library behind it — capacity plus a repeatable, measured system.
cards.objection: Isn't a managed service just reselling engineering hours?
cards.reframe: Not when it's built on a methodology you proved internally and a growing playbook library. Clients aren't buying hours — they're buying a repeatable system with ROI analytics, which is exactly what a staffing shop can't offer.
cards.proofMetric: A growing number of AHEAD clients now run Devin as an ongoing managed service, on the same methodology AHEAD proved on itself

personaShift.CTO: differentiates on methodology, not raw capacity
personaShift.VPE: delivers a repeatable managed system, not ad-hoc hours
personaShift.CFO: defends pricing with a proven, measured offering
personaShift.CRO: moves the deal up-market from staffing to strategic partner

cards.soWhat: A managed service without methodology is body-shopping. Differentiate on the repeatable system you proved on yourself.

cards.prompt: One of our engineers built a great agent workflow, but it's stuck on their team. How do we spread a single champion's win across the whole org?
cards.rootCause: High-value workflows stay trapped with their author unless they're made portable.
cards.consequence: Wins don't compound — each team keeps reinventing what a peer already solved.
cards.diagnostic: Which of your best agent workflows could another squad run tomorrow with just a shared playbook and minimal config?
cards.angle: Package a champion's workflow as a shareable playbook so any squad can replicate it against its own repos — turning one team's investment into a platform capability.
cards.objection: Every team's setup is different, so workflows won't transfer.
cards.reframe: The context differs, but the workflow is portable by design. One person invests the time to build the playbook with the right context; the whole org runs it with minimal config. A single champion's win becomes everyone's baseline.
cards.proofMetric: Evinova's AutoFixer and RV Tech's SIL playbook were built once and replicated across squads — one team's investment became a platform capability

personaShift.CTO: turns isolated wins into org-wide platform capability
personaShift.VPE: lets every squad reuse a proven workflow, not rebuild it
personaShift.CFO: amortizes one champion's effort across many teams
personaShift.CRO: shows compounding, portable value across the account

cards.soWhat: A champion's workflow is worthless if it's stuck on one team. Make it a portable playbook and one win becomes everyone's baseline.
